Bearing 315

The Newsletter of Orienteering in the Pacific Northwest | Winter 2007

WIOL

Jim Siscel, WIOL Director

Each year the league gets better. We have 211 registered WIOL participants—186 season and 25 day of meet. We have teams from fifteen schools and individuals from fifteen schools. We are averaging 142 participants for the first four meets. The participants are seeing some new venues this year, which is a challenge that they all seem to be meeting very well. The 25th year Championships will be at Point Defiance Park on February 17th. Come out, run your adult course and cheer these young orienteers as they cross the finish line.

President’s Comments

Jim Siscel, Cascade President

We have had a great fall orienteering season. At the National Orienteering Day at Woodland Park we had over 135 participants, including many newcomers the whole point of the meet. The next meet was on a new map produced by Mike Schuh at Hyak Lake above Snoqualmie Pass. Oh what a day, wet, cold, and miserable, but 84 hardy orienteers attempted to complete four great courses. The weather contributed to the highest number of Did Not Finish (DNF) results, including myself, that I have ever seen. Hopefully the weather will cooperate for the next meet at Hyak Lake. At our annual Founder’s Day Meet at Ft. Casey the weather improved and we had 139 participants. A family combination Jana Dvorak (mother), Len Englund award, and Matej Urban (son), Bo Powell award, received the two awards named for two of the club’s founders. October 29 saw the Vampires out in Lynndale Park for the Vampire O. The weather cooperated again, cold but no rain. There were 174 participants out in the dark trying not to be bitten. Jana and Matej found all 24 controls in just under 36 minutes and they were bitten by a Vampire. They have the task of setting the controls for next year’s Vampire O.

November 4th saw the start of the Winter O/WIOL (Washington Interscholastic Orienteering League) season. The first meet at Lincoln Park was blowing and raining, but that did not stop the 132 hardy Puget Sound orienteers on the Public Courses. On to Magnuson Park for the next meet. This was the first time we have had a meet at Magnuson since 1999. Weather cooperated and we had to my knowledge the largest attendance at a local meet ever with 194 on the public courses plus the 161 WIOL participants. North Sea-Tac map, another new map by Mike Schuh, saw a gloomy foggy day at the start and a bright sunny day at the end. With the fog if you didn’t have your compass you could tell which way was North by listening to the airplanes landing at SeaTac. They were landing north to south and we were right under both landing paths for the two runways at SeaTac. Winter O #4 saw us at a non-park venue at the University of Washington. Under the circumstances that the previous Thursday was the Big Windstorm and no power for many of us we had a good turnout. The weather was good and maybe you were tired of no power and thought this was a good way to get warm.

In my September Comments, I noted that in order to run these large meets that we need to have many volunteers. For the four Winter O/WIOL meets we have had so far we have had 49 members volunteer to serve for the minimum 1 ½ hours. 27 of these volunteer have helped at two or more meets or two or more positions at one meet. We have a membership of 300 counting individuals as 1 and families as 2. We need to have more people volunteering, so the 27 don’t have to work three or more times in the future. Remember if you volunteer for 1½ hours you receive a Appreciation Coupon that allows you to attend a future meet for FREE. Please when any meet director asks for volunteers step forward. This is a great way to meet the other members of the club as they come through your position. The meet directors ask for volunteers using our Cascade O List. If you are not a member you can become one by going to our website where on the home page near the bottom you will find the link to become a member. If you do not have email, check at the Registration Area for the volunteer sign-up list.

Earlier the Board decided that the club would require all WIOL participants to have a whistle for safety when competing this year. The Board revisited this idea at the December meeting and has decided that at all COC meets ALL participants will be required to have a whistle for safety. Starting with the first meet in March 2007 this policy will be implemented. The club has purchased whistles and will provide one for free to those that do not have them.

Tips & Techniques

The Run vs. Walk Decision: Some Insight from Parade Magazine
(Free with the Sunday Times/PI)
Don Atkinson, Kenmore

If we’re fit enough, we don’t need to make this decision, we can run the whole course. But some (most?) of us can’t do that, so the question is, “when is it best to take a break and walk for a while”?

Several years ago, I read an article in Orienteering North America that suggested that taking a break on hills was the best solution, but without going into the details. A couple of weeks ago, Marilyn vos Savant’s column in Parade magazine indirectly addressed this issue with a quiz about how much time is gained by running rather than walking. It did start me thinking about the question again. Here’s the item.

"Ron walks a one-hour round trip from his house and back each day. One day, he decides to run part of the way. Ron runs three times as fast as he walks. If he cut 10 minutes off his usual trip, how many minutes did Ron run? -
This can be solved at a glance with a certain insight. Readers who want to search for it should stop reading here."

Her answer continues at the end of this article.

On a gentle downhill, my running speed is three to four times my walking speed. On an uphill section, the ratio is roughly twice the walking speed. From basic common sense, and with numbers from the Vos Savant item, its clear that the bigger the ratio between running and walking speeds, the more fatal it is to your elapsed time to walk, rather than run. So it is clear, that you should avoid slowing down on the fast sections of the course unless you just can’t keep up the pace, or are worried about making a navigation mistake.

The ONA comment is correct, if you have to, it’s best to take a walk break on the uphill section where the speed ratio is least. That’s when you need the break most anyway. When you do slow down, make the most of it by reading the map, and planning your next leg.

Now back to the final paragraph of the Vos Savant item:

Here’s the insight: If Ron cut 10 minutes from his trip, he must have run for half that time. Why is this so? Ron runs three times as fast as he walks. If he takes three minutes to walk a distance, he takes one minute to run that same distance. So he gains two minutes of time for every minute he runs. Which means that if he cut 10 minutes from his trip, Ron ran for five minutes.

Successful Orienteering
from the WIOL Newsletter
January 14, 1989

  1. Successful orienteering requires the use of skills and strategies that maximize your abilities to find the controls and minimize your errors.
  2. To select a route, first select an attack point, a distinct feature near the control from which you can find the control easily. Second, select a safe route to the attack point.
  3. Good attack points are distinct, easy to find features, close to the control (within 200 to 300 meters) and afford the best possible view of the terrain.
  4. Aiming off is a strategy used to simplify a route by purposely selecting a route to one side of your goal so that you are sure of the direction of your turn.
  5. Attacking the control from above gives you the best view of both the control and the best approach to it.
  6. Climbing in the beginning of a leg lessens the probability of error caused by poor field of vision and fatigue that comes with climbing at the end of the leg.
  7. Contouring around large hills and deep valleys save time and energy.
  8. Plan where you want to go, what you should see in the terrain and what strategies you will use to find the control
  9. Follow your plan, unless it becomes inappropriate because of the map or terrain, at which time you should select a new plan.

block_index.text: The Newsletter of Orienteering in the Pacific Northwest | Winter 2007

Each year the league gets better. We have 211 registered WIOL participants—186 season and 25 day of meet. We have teams from fifteen schools and individuals from fifteen schools. We are averaging 142 participants for the first four meets. The participants are seeing some new venues this year, which is a challenge that they all seem to be meeting very well. The 25th year Championships will be at Point Defiance Park on February 17th. Come out, run your adult course and cheer these young orienteers as they cross the finish line.

We have had a great fall orienteering season. At the National Orienteering Day at Woodland Park we had over 135 participants, including many newcomers the whole point of the meet. The next meet was on a new map produced by Mike Schuh at Hyak Lake above Snoqualmie Pass. Oh what a day, wet, cold, and miserable, but 84 hardy orienteers attempted to complete four great courses. The weather contributed to the highest number of Did Not Finish (DNF) results, including myself, that I have ever seen. Hopefully the weather will cooperate for the next meet at Hyak Lake. At our annual Founder’s Day Meet at Ft. Casey the weather improved and we had 139 participants. A family combination Jana Dvorak (mother), Len Englund award, and Matej Urban (son), Bo Powell award, received the two awards named for two of the club’s founders. October 29 saw the Vampires out in Lynndale Park for the Vampire O. The weather cooperated again, cold but no rain. There were 174 participants out in the dark trying not to be bitten. Jana and Matej found all 24 controls in just under 36 minutes and they were bitten by a Vampire. They have the task of setting the controls for next year’s Vampire O.

November 4th saw the start of the Winter O/WIOL (Washington Interscholastic Orienteering League) season. The first meet at Lincoln Park was blowing and raining, but that did not stop the 132 hardy Puget Sound orienteers on the Public Courses. On to Magnuson Park for the next meet. This was the first time we have had a meet at Magnuson since 1999. Weather cooperated and we had to my knowledge the largest attendance at a local meet ever with 194 on the public courses plus the 161 WIOL participants. North Sea-Tac map, another new map by Mike Schuh, saw a gloomy foggy day at the start and a bright sunny day at the end. With the fog if you didn’t have your compass you could tell which way was North by listening to the airplanes landing at SeaTac. They were landing north to south and we were right under both landing paths for the two runways at SeaTac. Winter O #4 saw us at a non-park venue at the University of Washington. Under the circumstances that the previous Thursday was the Big Windstorm and no power for many of us we had a good turnout. The weather was good and maybe you were tired of no power and thought this was a good way to get warm.

In my September Comments, I noted that in order to run these large meets that we need to have many volunteers. For the four Winter O/WIOL meets we have had so far we have had 49 members volunteer to serve for the minimum 1 ½ hours. 27 of these volunteer have helped at two or more meets or two or more positions at one meet. We have a membership of 300 counting individuals as 1 and families as 2. We need to have more people volunteering, so the 27 don’t have to work three or more times in the future. Remember if you volunteer for 1½ hours you receive a Appreciation Coupon that allows you to attend a future meet for FREE. Please when any meet director asks for volunteers step forward. This is a great way to meet the other members of the club as they come through your position. The meet directors ask for volunteers using our Cascade O List. If you are not a member you can become one by going to our website where on the home page near the bottom you will find the link to become a member. If you do not have email, check at the Registration Area for the volunteer sign-up list.

Earlier the Board decided that the club would require all WIOL participants to have a whistle for safety when competing this year. The Board revisited this idea at the December meeting and has decided that at all COC meets ALL participants will be required to have a whistle for safety. Starting with the first meet in March 2007 this policy will be implemented. The club has purchased whistles and will provide one for free to those that do not have them.

The Run vs. Walk Decision: Some Insight from Parade Magazine (Free with the Sunday Times/PI) Don Atkinson, Kenmore

If we’re fit enough, we don’t need to make this decision, we can run the whole course. But some (most?) of us can’t do that, so the question is, “when is it best to take a break and walk for a while”?

Several years ago, I read an article in Orienteering North America that suggested that taking a break on hills was the best solution, but without going into the details. A couple of weeks ago, Marilyn vos Savant’s column in Parade magazine indirectly addressed this issue with a quiz about how much time is gained by running rather than walking. It did start me thinking about the question again. Here’s the item.

"Ron walks a one-hour round trip from his house and back each day. One day, he decides to run part of the way. Ron runs three times as fast as he walks. If he cut 10 minutes off his usual trip, how many minutes did Ron run? - This can be solved at a glance with a certain insight. Readers who want to search for it should stop reading here."
Her answer continues at the end of this article.

On a gentle downhill, my running speed is three to four times my walking speed. On an uphill section, the ratio is roughly twice the walking speed. From basic common sense, and with numbers from the Vos Savant item, its clear that the bigger the ratio between running and walking speeds, the more fatal it is to your elapsed time to walk, rather than run. So it is clear, that you should avoid slowing down on the fast sections of the course unless you just can’t keep up the pace, or are worried about making a navigation mistake.

The ONA comment is correct, if you have to, it’s best to take a walk break on the uphill section where the speed ratio is least. That’s when you need the break most anyway. When you do slow down, make the most of it by reading the map, and planning your next leg.
Now back to the final paragraph of the Vos Savant item:

Here’s the insight: If Ron cut 10 minutes from his trip, he must have run for half that time. Why is this so? Ron runs three times as fast as he walks. If he takes three minutes to walk a distance, he takes one minute to run that same distance. So he gains two minutes of time for every minute he runs. Which means that if he cut 10 minutes from his trip, Ron ran for five minutes.
Successful Orienteering from the WIOL Newsletter January 14, 1989
Successful orienteering requires the use of skills and strategies that maximize your abilities to find the controls and minimize your errors.
To select a route, first select an attack point, a distinct feature near the control from which you can find the control easily. Second, select a safe route to the attack point.
Good attack points are distinct, easy to find features, close to the control (within 200 to 300 meters) and afford the best possible view of the terrain.
Aiming off is a strategy used to simplify a route by purposely selecting a route to one side of your goal so that you are sure of the direction of your turn.
Attacking the control from above gives you the best view of both the control and the best approach to it.
Climbing in the beginning of a leg lessens the probability of error caused by poor field of vision and fatigue that comes with climbing at the end of the leg.
Contouring around large hills and deep valleys save time and energy.
Plan where you want to go, what you should see in the terrain and what strategies you will use to find the control
Follow your plan, unless it becomes inappropriate because of the map or terrain, at which time you should select a new plan.
